1222	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
1223	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1224	  Restrict attention to \(\otimes\)-invertible objects. Closure holds: if
1225	  \(M, N\) are \(\otimes\)-invertible with inverses \(M', N'\), then
1226	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X (M' \otimes_X N') \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the
1227	  coherence isomorphisms, so \(M \otimes_X N\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible.
1228	  Well-definedness of \([M]\cdot[N] := [M \otimes_X N]\) on iso-classes is the
1229	  bifunctoriality of \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}. The
1230	  group axioms are propositions about iso-classes and are discharged by the
1231	  coherence isomorphisms, each evaluated on the invertible objects in play:
1232	  associativity by \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (whose hypotheses are exactly
1233	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility of the three arguments), the unit laws by
1234	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity by
1235	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}; no pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
1236	  invoked, since each axiom asserts only the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism.
1237	  Every class \([M]\) has an inverse \([N]\) with
1238	  \([M \otimes_X N] = [\mathcal{O}_X]\), namely the tensor inverse carried by
1239	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}).
1240	  Therefore the iso-classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible modules form an abelian
1241	  group --- the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- equivalently the
1242	  group of units of the (un-built) commutative monoid of all iso-classes. The
1243	  whole assembly follows the Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\)
1244	  template.
1245	\end{proof}
1246	
1247	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
1248	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
1249	
1250	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
1251	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
1252	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
1253	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1254	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
1255	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
1256	
1257	\begin{theorem}
1258	
1259	\leanok
1260	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
1261	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
1262	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
1263	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1264	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1265	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1266	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1267	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1268	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
1269	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
1270	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1271	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1272	
1273	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
1274	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
1275	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
1276	  % groups)
1277	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
1278	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1279	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
1280	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
1281	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
1282	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
1283	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
1284	  % \end{dfn}
1285	  %  [\dots]
1286	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
1287	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
1288	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
1289	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1290	  % topologies  by
1291	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1292	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1293	  %  \end{dfn}"
1294	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1295	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1296	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1297	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1298	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1299	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1300	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1301	  \[
1302	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1303	  \]
1304	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1305	  abelian-group structure with addition
1306	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1307	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1308	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1309	  inverse
1310	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1311	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1312	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1313	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1314	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1315	  map
1316	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1317	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1318	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1319	\end{theorem}
1320	
1321	\begin{proof}
1322	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1323	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1324	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1325	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1326	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1327	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1328	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1329	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1330	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1331	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1332	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1333	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1334	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1335	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1336	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1337	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1338	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1339	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1340	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1341	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1342	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1343	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1344	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1345	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1346	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1347	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1348	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1349	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1350	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1351	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1352	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1353	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1354	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1355	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1356	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1357	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1358	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1359	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1360	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1361	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1362	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1363	
1364	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1365	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1366	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1367	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1368	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1369	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1370	
1371	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1372	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1373	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1374	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1375	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1376	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1377	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1378	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1379	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1380	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1381	\end{proof}

21	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). Per the iter-174 plan, the chapter has
22	% two responsibilities. First, package the abelian-group structure on the

25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}

150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.

156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)

165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}

176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[

185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}

228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
230	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map

239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
241	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument

257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
261	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map
264	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
265	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By

268	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
269	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
270	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
271	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
272	\end{proof}

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.

485	% (\(\langle 0\rangle : \texttt{Nonempty}\,(\texttt{presheaf}\ C
486	%   \Rightarrow \texttt{etSheaf}.\mathrm{obj})\)),
487	% NOT the canonical sheafification unit. The Lean statement type has
488	% been weakened to bare \texttt{Nonempty} existence to admit this
489	% trivial witness; the canonical morphism with its universal property
490	% is the content of \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical} and
491	% is deferred. The closure is gated on the upstream Mathlib
492	% \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO NOT promote this
493	% block to \leanok-on-the-canonical-statement via the deterministic
494	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in review
495	% beyond the weak \texttt{Nonempty} content, until the body is
496	% replaced with the canonical sheafification unit and the prose is
497	% upgraded accordingly.
498	\begin{proof}
499	
500	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
501	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
502	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
503	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the
504	  hom-set of presheaf morphisms between these two objects is therefore
505	  nonempty. The full universal property of this canonical morphism
506	  --- and consequently the claim that the abelian-group structure on
507	  the sheafification is uniquely pinned by it --- is the content of
508	  the forward-looking \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
509	  below; see its proof sketch for the canonical formulation.
510	\end{proof}
511	
512	\begin{theorem}[Canonical sheafification unit with universal property (forward-looking)]
513	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_unit_canonical}
514	
515	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
516	  % Def.~df:Pfs + the associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following
517	  % (the \'etale sheaf is characterised as the sheaf associated to the
518	  % relative Picard functor, via the natural map from a presheaf to its
519	  % associated sheaf --- i.e.\ the sheafification unit with its universal
520	  % property)
521	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1328).
522	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
523	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
524	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
525	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
526	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
527	  % topologies  by
528	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
529	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
530	  %  \end{dfn}
531	  %  [\dots]
532	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
533	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
534	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
535	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
536	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
537	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
538	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
539	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
540	  Def.~df:Pfs together with the associated-sheaf paragraph
541	  immediately following (the natural map from a presheaf to its
542	  associated sheaf, taken inside the category of abelian groups, is
543	  universal among presheaf morphisms from \(\Pic_{X/S}\) into \'etale
544	  sheaves of abelian groups).}
545	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. There
546	  exists a canonical morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
547	  \[
548	    \eta \;\colon\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\;
549	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}
550	  \]
551	  with the following universal property. For every \'etale sheaf
552	  \(\mathcal{F}\) of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and every morphism
553	  of presheaves of abelian groups
554	  \(\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F}\), there is a unique
555	  morphism of \'etale sheaves of abelian groups
556	  \(\tilde\varphi : \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \mathcal{F}\) such that
557	  \(\tilde\varphi \circ \eta = \varphi\). Equivalently, the pair
558	  \(\bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}, \, \eta\bigr)\) is initial among
559	  pairs \((\mathcal{F}, \, \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \mathcal{F})\)
560	  consisting of an \'etale sheaf of abelian groups on \((\Sch/k)\) and
561	  a morphism of presheaves of abelian groups from \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\)
562	  to \(\mathcal{F}\). In particular, the abelian-group structure on
563	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) at each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) is uniquely
564	  determined (up to canonical isomorphism) by the abelian-group
565	  structure on \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) and the universal property of
566	  \(\eta\).
567	\end{theorem}
568	
569	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs itself, Kleiman \S 2 does not
570	% supply a separate proof body --- the universal property is built
571	% into ``the sheaf associated to'' as a synonym for sheafification,
572	% as the verbatim quote above already makes plain. The project-side
573	% argument is the standard universal property of sheafification in a
574	% Grothendieck topology, transported through the colimit-preservation
575	% of \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\).
576	\begin{proof}
577	
578	  In any Grothendieck topology, every presheaf has a canonical
579	  associated sheaf, and the canonical morphism from a presheaf to its
580	  associated sheaf is universal among morphisms from the presheaf into
581	  sheaves (Stacks tag 00WI; Kleiman \S 2, paragraph following
582	  df:Pfs). The \'etale site of \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck
583	  topology, so this applies in particular to \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) and
584	  yields the canonical set-valued sheafification
585	  \((\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) together with the universal arrow
586	  out of \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\). The forgetful functor
587	  \(\AddCommGroup \to \mathbf{Set}\) preserves and reflects the
588	  filtered colimits and finite limits used by the plus-construction,
589	  so performing the same plus-construction inside \(\AddCommGroup\)
590	  yields a sheaf of abelian groups whose underlying set-valued sheaf
591	  coincides with the set-valued sheafification; the abelian-group
592	  structure on the sheafification is transported back through this
593	  identification. The resulting morphism \(\eta\) is then by
594	  construction the universal arrow in the abelian-group-valued
595	  sheafification, which is the universal property in the form stated.
596	\end{proof}
597

701	\paragraph{Gate annotation (iter-198 refresh).}
702	The residual prover gate inside
703	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean} (the file
704	implementing the six declarations above) is \emph{not} on the
705	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier --- that typed sorry was closed
706	in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean} during
707	the iter-188 unpacking, where
708	\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} was concretised as the subtype
709	\(\{N : (\texttt{pullback}\ \pi_C\ \pi_T).\mathtt{Modules}\ \mathbin{|}\
710	\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\ N\}\) and
711	\texttt{pullbackAlongProjection} / \texttt{pullback\_pullback\_eq} were
712	filled axiom-cleanly using \texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullback},
713	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}, and
714	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}.
715	
716	The genuine residual gate is the abelian-group instance on the
717	quotient \texttt{Quotient (preimage\_subgroup πC πT)} (the file-local
718	sorry scaffold near \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L205--L235, which the
719	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup} declaration is
720	pinned to). That instance in turn depends on a tensor-product
721	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure on \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}:
722	forming the abelian-group law on isomorphism classes
723	\([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes L']\) requires a tensor-product operation
724	on the carrier and an inverse for invertibles. Mathlib at the pinned
725	commit \texttt{b80f227} ships
726	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} (a tensor product at
727	the level of \texttt{PresheafOfModules}), but does \emph{not} expose a
728	monoidal-category structure on the higher-level
729	\texttt{Scheme.Modules} category; this is the gap explicitly flagged
730	in \texttt{LineBundlePullback.lean}, L344--L346, with the comment
731	``Mathlib ships \texttt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj} but no
732	monoidal structure on \texttt{Scheme.Modules} directly at
733	\texttt{b80f227}'' --- the \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
734	gap.
735	
736	Modulo that single file-local sorry, the five other prover-gate
737	sorries at \texttt{RelPicFunctor.lean} L287--L482 (the bodies of
738	\texttt{PicSharp},
739	\texttt{PicSharp.functorial},
740	\texttt{PicSharp.presheaf},
741	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf}, and
742	\texttt{PicSharp.etSheaf\_group\_structure}) close axiom-cleanly: each
743	consumes \texttt{addCommGroup} as a typeclass hypothesis and threads
744	the resulting group structure through the standard Mathlib pattern
745	(\texttt{QuotientAddGroup} for the quotient, \texttt{Functor.mk} for
746	the presheaf packaging, \texttt{Quotient.lift} respecting the
747	\texttt{AddCommGroup} structure for the functorial action, and
748	\texttt{CategoryTheory.presheafToSheaf etaleTopology AddCommGrpCat}
749	for the \'etale sheafification with its abelian-group target).
750	Once the \texttt{addCommGroup} instance is provided, the file
751	collapses from six sorries to one, and the one remaining sorry is
752	gated only on the Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure
753	gap rather than on any project-side mathematics (making it an
754	upstream Mathlib-build-mode task, not an A.1.c mathematical gap).
755	
756	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
757	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
758	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
759	\(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)''), \emph{not} \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) directly. The \'etale
760	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the
761	representability theorem in A.2.c well-posed.

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	
360	\leanok
361	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
362	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
363	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
364	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
365	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
366	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
367	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
368	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
369	  closed embedding.
370	
371	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
372	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
373	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
374	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
375	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
376	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
377	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
378	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
379	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
380	
381	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
382	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
383	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
384	
385	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
386	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
387	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
388	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
389	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
390	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
391	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
392	\end{proof}

394	\section{Group-scheme structure}
395	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
396	
397	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
398	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
399	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
400	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
401	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
402	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
403	
404	\begin{theorem}\leanok
405	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
406	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
408	  
409	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
410	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
411	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
412	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
413	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
414	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
415	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
416	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
417	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
418	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
419	  %  topologies  by
420	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
421	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
422	  %   \end{dfn}"
423	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
424	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
425	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
426	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
427	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
428	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
429	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
430	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
431	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
432	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
433	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
434	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
435	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
436	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
437	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
438	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
439	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
440	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
441	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
442	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
443	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
444	  scheme locally of finite type.
445	\end{theorem}
446	
447	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
448	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
449	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
450	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
451	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
452	% records the standard transfer.
453	\begin{proof}
454	  
455	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
456	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
457	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
458	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
459	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
460	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
461	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
462	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
463	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
464	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
465	  in the value category).
466	
467	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
468	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
469	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
470	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
471	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
472	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
473	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
474	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
475	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
476	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
477	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
478	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
479	\end{proof}

481	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
482	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
483	
484	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
485	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
486	
487	\begin{definition}\leanok
488	[The Picard scheme]
489	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
491	  
492	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
493	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
494	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
497	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
498	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
499	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
500	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
501	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch.}
502	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
503	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing
504	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\), supplied by
505	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, equipped with the abelian-group-scheme
506	  structure of \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}. The scheme is separated,
507	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open
508	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes.
509	\end{definition}
510	
511	\section{Lean encoding}
512	\label{sec:fga_pic_lean_encoding}
513	
514	The Lean target file is the new
515	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}, sibling to
516	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
517	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) and
518	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
519	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}). The blueprint declarations of
520	this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
521	
522	\begin{itemize}
523	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
524	    (\cref{def:pic_scheme}) is a structural constant naming the scheme
525	    representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); it is the witness extracted
526	    from the \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy} produced by
527	    \texttt{representable} below.
528	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
529	    (\cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}) is the morphism of
530	    \(k\)-schemes \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) sending a
531	    relative effective divisor to its associated invertible sheaf.
532	    The construction uses the Hilbert / Quot scheme produced by
533	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (named
534	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotScheme} per the directive's
535	    sibling-chapter naming), restricts to the open subscheme of relative
536	    effective divisors via the Kleiman~\S 3 effective-divisor open
537	    criterion, and composes with the quotient map to
538	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
539	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
540	    (\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}) is the Altman--Kleiman
541	    descent of a flat-and-proper equivalence relation $R \subseteq Z
542	    \times_k Z\( on a quasi-projective \)k\(-scheme \)Z$ to a
543	    quasi-projective quotient \(Q\), packaged in the form that consumes
544	    a \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for \(Z\) and produces
545	    one for the coequalising sheaf.
546	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
547	    (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}) is the assembly: from the
548	    \texttt{abelMap}, the \texttt{QuotScheme} representability witness,
549	    and the \texttt{rel\_pic\_etale\_sheafification} of
550	    \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}, build the
551	    \(\Functor.\mathrm{RepresentableBy}\)-witness for
552	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) via the four steps of the proof above
553	    (Hilbert-polynomial stratification, \(m\)-regularity bound, Abel-map
554	    factorisation, smooth-proper quotient).
555	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
556	    (\cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}) is the
557	    \texttt{CommGroupObject} (or
558	    \texttt{AddCommGrp\_Object}) instance on \texttt{PicScheme} obtained
559	    by Yoneda transport of the abelian-group structure on
560	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\).
561	\end{itemize}
562	
563	No new core axiom is required: this chapter is an assembly chapter on
564	top of the QuotScheme engine, the relative-Picard \'etale sheafification,
565	and the line-bundle pullback machinery already in place. The single
566	large external input (the Altman--Kleiman theorem of effective
567	flat-and-proper equivalence relations on quasi-projective schemes) is
568	recorded as \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} and is itself a standard
569	result; if its Lean formalisation is not yet in Mathlib, the
570	\texttt{smoothProperQuotient} Lean declaration may be sorried and the
571	gap reported to the plan agent, as it is logically downstream of the
572	Hilbert-scheme construction of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.

574	\section{Sorry-by-sorry closure order}
575	\label{sec:fga_pic_sorry_closure_order}
576	
577	% iter-200 plan agent: stale-prose findings from iter-199
578	% lean-vs-blueprint-checker fga-iter199 addressed below (intro paragraph,
579	% Sorry 4 subsection location, closure-order summary Sorry 4 motivation).
580	The Lean target file
581	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} currently
582	carries seven open sorries, all of them \texttt{⟨sorry⟩} instance
583	bodies for the seven carrier typeclasses
584	\texttt{HasPicSharp}, \texttt{HasDivFunctor}, \texttt{HasPicScheme},
585	\texttt{HasAbelMap}, \texttt{HasSmoothProperQuotient},
586	\texttt{PicSharpRepresentable}, \texttt{PicSchemeGroupObject}.
587	After the iter-199 carrier-soundness refactor the file is
588	structurally homogeneous: every sorry is isolated in a single
589	\texttt{⟨sorry⟩} instance constructor of a \texttt{Prop}-valued
590	typeclass, so every theorem body extracts cleanly from the
591	appropriate field of the corresponding typeclass and is itself
592	axiom-clean. The carrier-soundness refactor of the
593	preceding section isolates each typeclass instance as the unique
594	sorry-carrying site for its associated extracted definition, so a
595	consumer that abstracts over the typeclass is kernel-clean. This
596	section is the explicit closure-order plan: for each sorry we record
597	(i) the Lean location and identifier, (ii) the mathematical content
598	to be proved, (iii) the Mathlib prerequisites at the project's pinned
599	revision, (iv) the Route~C (Riemann--Roch) substrate dependency, and
600	(v) a closure-order rank, where rank~1 is an independent carrier that
601	can be discharged from existing Mathlib alone, rank~2 depends on a
602	prior carrier or a Mathlib gap that is not Riemann--Roch, and rank~3
603	depends on a Route~C substrate re-engagement.
604	
605	\medskip
606	
607	The ranks are not a serialisation: rank-1 carriers may be closed in
608	parallel; rank-2 and rank-3 carriers depend either on other carriers
609	in the same chapter, on sibling A.1.c / A.2.b chapters
610	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
611	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}), or on the Route~C arc of the project
612	strategy (the Riemann--Roch development for smooth proper curves over
613	a field). The strategic point of the ranks is to identify which
614	sorries can be attempted first while the Route~C arc remains
615	substrate-blocked.

797	\paragraph{Mathematical content.} The smooth-proper quotient lemma
798	of \cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}: given a surjection
799	$\alpha : Z \to P$ of \'etale sheaves on $(\Sch/k)^{op}$ with $Z$
800	quasi-projective, $R := Z \times_P Z$ representable, and the first
801	projection $R \to Z$ smooth and proper, the quotient $P$ is
802	representable by a quasi-projective $k$-scheme. The proof packages
803	the Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem for
804	flat-and-proper relations on quasi-projective schemes.
805	
806	\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
807	\begin{itemize}
808	  \item \texttt{Functor.IsRepresentable},
809	    \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Limits.pullback}:
810	    \emph{exists}.
811	  \item \texttt{Smooth}, \texttt{IsProper} typeclasses on scheme
812	    morphisms: \emph{exists}.
813	  \item EGA IV~8.11.5 "proper monomorphism is a closed immersion":
814	    \emph{missing}. Mathlib has closed immersions, monomorphisms of
815	    schemes, and properness, but the EGA combination lemma is not yet
816	    isolated as a named result.
817	  \item Altman--Kleiman effective-equivalence-relation theorem
818	    (flat-and-proper equivalence relation on a quasi-projective
819	    scheme is effective, with quasi-projective quotient):
820	    \emph{missing}. This is the structural heart of the lemma and is
821	    not in Mathlib in any form.
822	  \item Coequaliser uniqueness in the category of \'etale sheaves:
823	    \emph{exists} (general categorical fact, uniqueness up to unique
824	    isomorphism of coequalisers in any category).
825	\end{itemize}

829	independent of curves and Riemann--Roch. It does, however, sit on the

831	substantial development in its own right (corresponds to roughly
832	Altman--Kleiman, "Compactifying the Picard scheme", and the

834	
835	\paragraph{Closure recipe (iter-199+).} Two routes are available:
836	\begin{enumerate}
