208	\begin{lemma}
209	[Stacks 037Q iff-direction: connected + section ⟹ geometrically connected]
210	  \label{lem:geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
211	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section}
212	  % SOURCE: [Stacks], tag 037Q (criteria for geometric connectedness via
213	  % algebraic closure of the base field in the global sections) +
214	  % tag 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE: Stacks 037Q (Lemma): "Let \(X\) be a scheme over the
216	  % field \(k\). Set \(K = H^0(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The following are
217	  % equivalent: (1) \(X\) is geometrically connected over \(k\), (2)
218	  % \(X\) is connected and \(k\) is algebraically closed in \(K\), (3)
219	  % \(K\) is a field and the inclusion \(k \subset K\) is purely
220	  % inseparable."  (read from references/stacks-varieties.tex section
221	  % \S geom-connected, tag 037Q).
222	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)).}
223	  Let \(k\) be a field and let \(f : X \to \Spec k\) be a morphism of
224	  schemes such that the underlying topological space \(|X|\) is
225	  connected. Suppose there is a \(k\)-rational section \(s : \Spec k \to X\)
226	  of \(f\) (so \(s \circ f = \id_X\) topologically and the image of \(s\)
227	  meets every irreducible component of \(X\) through which \(s\) factors).
228	  Then \(f : X \to \Spec k\) is geometrically connected, in the sense
229	  that for every field extension \(K/k\) the base change \(X_K\) is
230	  connected.
231	\end{lemma}
232	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Stacks 037Q proof, abridged: ``(2) ⟹ (1).  By
233	% Lemma 04KV, it suffices to show that \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
234	% for every finite separable field extension \(k'/k\).  By Lemma 0359
235	% \(X \times_k k' = \Spec(k') \times_{\Spec k} X\) and \(H^0(X
236	% \times_k k', \mathcal{O}) = K \otimes_k k'\).  Since \(k\) is
237	% algebraically closed in \(K\), the ring \(K \otimes_k k'\) is a
238	% field (a Henselian-type criterion); a scheme whose global section
239	% ring is a field is connected.''
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
242	  Following Stacks 037Q (iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1)) combined
243	  with Stacks 04KV (geometrically connected descent of clopen partitions).
244	  By Stacks 04KV it suffices to prove \(X \times_k k'\) is connected
245	  for every finite separable extension \(k'/k\). The
246	  \(k\)-rational section \(s\) gives a \(k'\)-rational point of
247	  \(X \times_k k'\) (namely \(s \otimes \id\)), so \(X \times_k k'\) is
248	  nonempty. Moreover, since \(s\) is a section of \(f\), the image of
249	  \(s \otimes \id\) hits every clopen partition of \(X \times_k k'\)
250	  that has a \(k'\)-fibre — but if \(X \times_k k'\) admits a nontrivial
251	  clopen partition \(X \times_k k' = U \sqcup V\), the section
252	  \(s \otimes \id\) lies in exactly one of \(U, V\); pulling back along
253	  the natural map \(X \times_k k' \to X\) gives a corresponding clopen
254	  partition of \(|X|\) (the image of \(U\) and the image of \(V\)
255	  are disjoint clopens in \(|X|\) by surjectivity of \(\Spec k' \to \Spec
256	  k\)). Since \(|X|\) is connected by hypothesis, one of those images is
257	  empty, contradicting non-triviality. The base change \(X \times_k k'\)
258	  is therefore connected.
259	
260	  Spelled out: this lemma is the iter-194 first-class formalisation of
261	  the Stacks 037Q iff-direction (2)\(\Rightarrow\)(1) specialised to the
262	  presence of a \(k\)-rational section (which automatically supplies
263	  the \enquote{algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(K\) equals \(k\)}
264	  condition via the section's pullback on global sections — see
265	  Stacks 037R for that step).
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	% Note: the lemma above is the substrate needed for
269	% thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes via the route described
270	% in the proof block below. iter-194+ prover work closes its body.
271	
272	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
273	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
274	% (3).)
275	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
276	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
277	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
278	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
279	% \(G^0\) is open too.
280	%
281	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
282	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with
283	% extending \(k\).
284	%
285	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
286	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
287	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
288	%
289	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
290	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
291	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
292	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
293	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
294	% [\dots]
295	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
296	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
297	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
298	% [\dots]
299	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
300	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
301	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
302	\begin{proof}
303	  We establish
304	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
305	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
306	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
307	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
308	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
309	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
310	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
311	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
312	
313	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
314	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
315	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
316	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
317	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
318	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
319	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
320	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
321	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
322	
323	  \emph{Base-change commutation
324	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
325	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
326	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
327	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
328	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
329	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
330	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
331	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
332	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
333	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
334	
335	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
336	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
337	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
338	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
339	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
340	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
341	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
342	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
343	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
344	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
345	
346	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
347	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
348	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
349	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
350	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
351	  \(\overline k\), the product
352	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
353	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
354	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
355	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
356	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
357	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
358	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
359	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
360	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
361	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
362	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
363	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
364	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
365	\end{proof}
366	
367	\section{The identity component of the Picard scheme}
368	\label{sec:pic0_definition}
369	
370	We now specialise the abstract identity-component construction to
371	\(G = \Pic_{C/k}\).
372	
373	\begin{definition}\leanok
374	[The identity component of the Picard scheme]
375	  \label{def:pic_zero_subscheme}
376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
377	  
378	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening + Prp.~prp:pic0;
379	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
380	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2836-L2841 +
381	  % L2921-L2929).
382	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
383	  % "Having treated the existence of the Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\), we now
384	  % turn to its structure.  In this section, we study the union
385	  % \(\IPicz_{X/S}\) of the connected components of the identity element,
386	  % \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\), for \(s\in S\)."
387	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
388	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
389	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
390	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
391	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
392	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
393	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
394	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
395	  % \(k\).
396	  %  \end{prp}"
397	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, opening +
398	  Prp.~prp:pic0.}
399	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
400	  \(k\). The \emph{identity component of the Picard scheme}
401	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the identity component
402	  \((\Pic_{C/k})^0\) of the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of
403	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}, in the sense of
404	  \cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}. By
405	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open
406	  and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) of finite type over \(k\),
407	  geometrically irreducible, and its formation commutes with
408	  extension of the base field.
409	\end{definition}
410	
411	\section{The degree map}
412	\label{sec:pic_degree}
413	
414	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
415	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
416	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
417	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
418	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
419	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
420	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
421	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
422	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
423	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
424	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
425	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
426	degree.
427	
428	\begin{definition}\leanok
429	[Degree of a Picard class]
430	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}
431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
432	  
433	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
434	  % \S III.1, ``Definitions and main statements'', p.~88 (PDF p.~94)
435	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf).
436	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
437	  % "Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over \(k\). The degree of a
438	  % divisor \(D = n_i P_i\) on \(C\) is \(n_i [k(P_i):k]\). Since every
439	  % invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is of the form \(\mathcal{L}(D)\)
440	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
441	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
442	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
443	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
444	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
445	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
446	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
447	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves
448	  % of degree zero on \(C\)."
449	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
450	  \S III.1, p.~88.}
451	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
452	  \(g\), and let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
453	  \cref{def:pic_scheme}. The \emph{degree map}
454	  \[
455	    \deg \;\colon\; \Pic_{C/k}(k) \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}
456	  \]
457	  is defined as follows: a \(k\)-point \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\)
458	  represents (after \'etale base change) an invertible sheaf
459	  \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\); its Hilbert polynomial with respect to a fixed
460	  degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\) is, by
461	  \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial} and Riemann--Roch,
462	  \[
463	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
464	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
465	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
466	  \]
467	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
468	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
469	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
470	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
471	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
472	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
473	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
474	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
475	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
476	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
477	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
478	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
479	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
480	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
481	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
482	  of degree \(d\).
483	\end{definition}
484	
485	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
486	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
487	
488	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
489	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
490	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
491	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
492	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
493	automatically.
494	
495	% The three conclusions of the original ``\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian
496	% variety of dimension \(g\)'' theorem are split below into per-conclusion
497	% blocks, each with its own \lean{...} pin. The Kleiman ex:jac + rmk:Jac
498	% verbatim source quote (covering the abelian-variety structure) is
499	% reproduced once at \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}; the Milne
500	% III.1.4(e) verbatim quote (covering the dimension formula) is at
501	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}; the Milne III.1 quote
502	% characterising \(\Pic^0(C)\) as degree-zero classes is already reproduced
503	% in \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} and referenced from
504	% \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}. The combined proof,
505	% sharing the \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:} comment, follows the third
506	% block.
507	
508	\begin{theorem}\leanok
509	[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
510	  \label{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}
511	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
512	
513	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
514	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~50--51 (read from
515	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3911-L3917 +
516	  % L3990-L3996).
517	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
518	  % "\begin{ex}\label{ex:jac}
519	  % Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat, its geometric fibers are integral
520	  % curves of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Show the
521	  % ``generalized Jacobians'' \(\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
522	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\).  And show this
523	  % family is projective if and only if \(X/S\) is smooth.
524	  % \end{ex}"
525	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
526	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
527	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
528	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
529	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
530	  % Exercise~REF."
531	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
532	  Ex.~ex:jac + Rmk.~rmk:Jac; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'',
533	  \S I.1 (def.\ of abelian variety, p.~8).}
534	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
535	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) of
536	  \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme} is an \emph{abelian variety over
537	  \(k\)}: it is proper, smooth, and geometrically irreducible as a
538	  \(k\)-scheme, and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme structure. (In the
539	  Lean encoding this is the conjunction of
540	  \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]}, and
541	  \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
542	  \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with the existence of a
543	  \texttt{[GrpObj]} structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\). Commutativity
544	  follows automatically from Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4 and is not
545	  separately stated.)
546	\end{theorem}
547	
548	\begin{theorem}
549	
550	\leanok
551	[Dimension of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals the genus of \(C\)]
552	  \label{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
554	
555	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
556	  % \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
557	  % PDF p.~92).
558	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
559	  % "(e) The dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\). If \(C\) has genus zero,
560	  % then \(\Jac(C) = 0\) (this is obvious, because \(\Pic^0(C) = 0\), even
561	  % when one goes to the algebraic closure). If \(C\) has genus 1, then
562	  % \(\Jac(C) = C\) (provided \(C\) has a rational point; otherwise it differs
563	  % from \(C\) --- because \(\Jac(C)\) always has a point)."
564	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
565	  \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86; cf.\ [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'',
566	  \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Ex.~ex:jac.}
567	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
568	  \(g = g(C)\) (\cref{def:genus}) over a field \(k\). The dimension of
569	  the abelian variety \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
570	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) equals the genus:
571	  \[
572	    \dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} \;=\; g(C).
573	  \]
574	\end{theorem}
575	
576	\begin{theorem}
577	
578	\leanok
579	[\(k\)-points of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) are the kernel of the degree map]
580	  \label{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}
581	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
582	
583	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
584	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at
585	  % \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} (read from
586	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~94).
587	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
588	  \S III.1, p.~88 (``\(\Pic^0(C)\)\dots\ degree zero''); the verbatim
589	  source quote is at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}.}
590	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
591	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
592	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
593	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
594	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup} applied to
595	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), specialised in \cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme})
596	  if and only if the degree
597	  \(\deg(\lambda) \in \mathbb{Z}\) of \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}
598	  vanishes. Equivalently,
599	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker\bigl(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\bigr)\).
600	\end{theorem}
601	
602	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (substrate construction --- the relevant source
603	% statements above prove the existence + properties; we assemble the
604	% conclusion from those statements together with the abstract
605	% identity-component substrate.)
606	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2939,
607	% Thm.~th:qpp&p, on smoothness/projectivity of \(\IPicz_{X/k}\):)
608	% "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
609	%  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
610	% and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
611	% quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
612	% \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective."
613	% (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3426,
614	% Cor.~cor:sm, dimension equals \(h^1(\mathcal O)\) when smooth:)
615	% "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
616	% \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
617	% \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
618	%  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
619	% \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
620	% everywhere."
621	\begin{proof}
622	  We establish
623	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety},
624	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}, and
625	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero} in turn, assembling
626	  the conclusions from the identity-component substrate of
627	  \cref{sec:identity_component_abstract} together with
628	  Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, Cor.~cor:sm, and Ex.~ex:jac, and
629	  Milne~\S I.1 Cor.~1.4 (commutativity) + Rmk.~III.1.4(e) (dimension).
630	
631	  \emph{Abelian-variety structure
632	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}).} We assemble the four
633	  conjuncts from four inputs.
634	
635	  (i) \emph{Clopen subgroup of finite type, geometrically irreducible.}
636	  By
637	  \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
638	  applied to \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite
639	  type by
640	  \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme,thm:fga_pic_representability}),
641	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open and closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
642	  of finite type over \(k\), geometrically irreducible, and its
643	  formation commutes with extending \(k\). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
644	  is separated and locally of finite type as a subscheme of
645	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\), and connected.
646	
647	  (ii) \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} The disjoint-union structure of
648	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union
649	  of open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) restricted to \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
650	  expresses the identity component as a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme
651	  (Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p: \(X/k\) projective and geometrically
652	  integral \(\Longrightarrow\) \(\Pic^0_{X/k}\) is quasi-projective).
653	
654	  (iii) \emph{Projectivity (properness).} For a smooth proper
655	  geometrically integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g > 0\), \(C/k\) is
656	  also geometrically normal (the smoothness implies geometric
657	  regularity, hence geometric normality). Kleiman~\S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p
658	  then upgrades the quasi-projective conclusion to projective:
659	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
660	  The proof of th:qpp\&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure
661	  theorem to reduce to ruling out non-constant maps
662	  \(\mathbb G_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), which on a geometrically normal
663	  \(X\) becomes a divisor-theoretic computation.
664	
665	  (iv) \emph{Smoothness.} For a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), the
666	  Picard scheme is smooth at \(0\): this is Exercise ex:jac of
667	  Kleiman~\S 5, which asserts that for \(X/S\) projective and flat
668	  with integral geometric fibres of arithmetic genus \(p_a\), the
669	  generalised Jacobians \(\Pic^0_{X_s/k_s}\) form a smooth
670	  quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a\); specialising
671	  to \(S = \Spec k\) and \(X = C\) a smooth proper curve of (geometric
672	  = arithmetic) genus \(g\) gives smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\).
673	
674	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, projective,
675	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-group scheme. A proper connected
676	  \(k\)-group variety is by definition an \emph{abelian variety}
677	  (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8), and is automatically commutative (Milne~\S I.1,
678	  Cor.~1.4). This establishes
679	  \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.
680	
681	  \emph{Dimension equals the genus
682	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}).} By Kleiman~\S 5,
683	  Cor.~cor:sm, the inequality
684	  \(\dim \Pic_{C/k} \le \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) holds, with
685	  equality if and only if \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\), in which
686	  case \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim_k H^1(C,
687	  \mathcal{O}_C)\) everywhere. Step (iv) above showed
688	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); equality therefore holds on
689	  the open identity component, and
690	  \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\)
691	  by \cref{def:genus}. This is Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): ``The
692	  dimension of \(J\) is the genus of \(C\)''.
693	
694	  \emph{Identification with the kernel of the degree map
695	  (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}).} A class
696	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the connected component of
697	  the identity --- i.e.\ in the image of the open immersion
698	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
699	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) --- precisely when
700	  the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf coincides
701	  with that of \(\mathcal{O}_C\), equivalently when its degree (the
702	  leading coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of
703	  \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is zero. Hence
704	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg : \Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z})\),
705	  which is the content of Milne~\S III.1, p.~88 (the verbatim quote
706	  at \cref{def:divisor_degree_pic} explicitly writes ``\(\Pic^0(C)\)
707	  for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves of degree
708	  zero on \(C\)'').
709	\end{proof}
710	
711	\section{Lean encoding}
712	\label{sec:pic0_lean_encoding}
713	
714	The Lean target file is
715	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/IdentityComponent.lean}, sibling to
716	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean}
717	(\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}). The blueprint declarations
718	of this chapter correspond to Lean targets as follows.
719	
720	\begin{itemize}
721	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent}
722	    (\cref{def:identity_component_group_scheme}) is a structural
723	    constant naming the open-and-closed identity-component subscheme
724	    of a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type. The construction
725	    is the abstract substrate of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps,
726	    independent of the Picard context; it is new project material
727	    (\emph{not} yet in Mathlib).
728	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme}
729	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}) packages the
730	    clopen-subscheme inclusion \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) (open immersion
731	    \emph{and} closed immersion) --- conclusion (a) of Kleiman~\S 5
732	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
733	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism}
734	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}) packages
735	    the group-homomorphism property of the inclusion of
736	    \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}: \(G^0\) inherits a
737	    \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\) compatible with the
738	    inclusion. Conclusion (b) of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3).
739	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible}
740	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible})
741	    packages the finite-type-ness and geometric-irreducibility
742	    conclusions of Kleiman~\S 5 Lem.~lem:agps~(3) (conclusion (c)).
743	    In Lean these are the instances
744	    \texttt{[LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom]},
745	    \texttt{[QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom]}, and
746	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom]}.
747	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso}
748	    (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}) packages the
749	    base-change-commutation conclusion (d) of Kleiman~\S 5
750	    Lem.~lem:agps~(3): for any field extension \(K/k\), the natural
751	    map \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) (in Lean,
752	    \texttt{(IdentityComponent G)\_K \(\to\) IdentityComponent (G\_K)})
753	    is an isomorphism of \(K\)-schemes.
754	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme}
755	    (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is the open subscheme of
756	    \(\Pic_{C/k}\) obtained by applying the
757	    \texttt{IdentityComponent} substrate to
758	    \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), inheriting the \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
759	    \cref{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}.
760	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree}
761	    (\cref{def:divisor_degree_pic}) is the group homomorphism
762	    \(\Pic_{C/k}(k) \to \mathbb{Z}\) extracting the leading coefficient
763	    of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
764	    relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on
765	    \(C\). On the disjoint-union decomposition of
766	    \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability} the degree is the index of the
767	    component.
768	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety}
769	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}) is the bundled
770	    abelian-variety structure on \texttt{Pic0Scheme}: the four-conjunct
771	    conjunction \texttt{[IsProper]}, \texttt{[Smooth]},
772	    \texttt{[GeometricallyIrreducible]} on the structural morphism
773	    \texttt{(Pic0Scheme C).hom} together with
774	    \texttt{Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C))}.
775	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.finrank_eq_genus}
776	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus}) is the dimension
777	    formula \(\dim_k \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\) (Milne~\S III.1,
778	    Rmk.~1.4(e)). In Lean this is the equation
779	    \texttt{Module.finrank k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C}
780	    (precise statement via the project's Krull-dimension API on the
781	    underlying scheme).
782	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.kPoints_iff_kerDegree}
783	    (\cref{thm:pic_zero_k_points_iff_degree_zero}) is the
784	    \(k\)-point-characterisation
785	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(k) = \ker(\deg)\) (Milne~\S III.1, p.~88). In Lean,
786	    \texttt{∀ λ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left,
787	    (∃ μ : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left,\,
788	    μ ≫ pic0Inclusion = λ) ↔ PicScheme.degree C λ = 0}.
789	\end{itemize}

791	The abstract identity-component substrate
792	(\texttt{GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} and its four
793	conclusion-specific theorems
794	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme},

796	\texttt{IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible},

807	\section{Out of scope}
808	\label{sec:pic0_out_of_scope}
809	
810	This chapter packages \emph{only} the identity component
811	\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an open-and-closed subgroup scheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\),
812	its degree-zero characterisation, and its abelian-variety structure.
813	The following downstream constructions live in separate sibling
814	chapters and are explicitly out of scope here:
815	
816	\begin{itemize}
817	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
818	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
819	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
820	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety}.

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}

273	  \[

275	  \]
276	  In particular, on an affine base the relative spectrum reduces to the absolute
277	  spectrum of the global sections.
278	\end{theorem}

317	  $\varphi_{\mathrm{univ}} : f_{\mathrm{univ}}^* \mathcal{A} = \widetilde{A \otimes_R A}
318	  \to \mathcal{O}_{\Spec A} = \widetilde A$ given by multiplication
319	  \(a \otimes a' \mapsto aa'\). We verify \((\Spec A, f_{\mathrm{univ}}, \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}})\)
320	  represents \(F\) on the affine base: given a test scheme \(T\) and a pair
321	  \((f, \varphi)\), the composite
322	  $A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{f^\sharp}
323	  \Gamma(T, f^*\mathcal{A}) \xrightarrow{\varphi} \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)$
324	  is a ring map \(A \to \Gamma(T, \mathcal{O}_T)\) and hence corresponds to a morphism
325	  \(T \to \Spec A\) by the affine universal property
326	  (Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.toSpec\_naturality} /
327	  \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toSpec}). One checks this assignment inverts the universal map
328	  \((f, \varphi) \mapsto a\) defined by \(f = f_{\mathrm{univ}} \circ a\),
329	  \(\varphi = a^* \varphi_{\mathrm{univ}}\).
330	\end{proof}

381	  
382	  \leanok
383	  % NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-179): the iter-185 Lane D HARD-BAR
384	  % closed BOTH iter-179 Mathlib-gap sorries kernel-clean:

400	  the functor \(F\) sending an \(X\)-scheme \(h : S \to X\) to the set of
401	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra maps \(h^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_S\). A morphism
402	  \(S \to T \times_X \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) over \(T\) is the same as a pair of an
403	  \(X\)-morphism \(S \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with a \(T\)-factorisation
404	  of \(S \to X\), i.e.\ a morphism \(S \to T\) whose composition with \(g\) equals

432	  %  \(S\)}, or simply the {\it spectrum of \(\mathcal{A}\) over \(S\)} is the scheme

453	  [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}

456	  \[
457	    \underline{\Spec}_X : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathrm{AffSch}/X
458	  \]

460	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras \(\psi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) induces, by the universal
461	  property (Theorem~REF) applied to the pair

479	  \pi_\mathcal{B}^*(\psi))\( defines an \)X$-morphism
480	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{B}) \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) via the
481	  representing universal pair. Identity and composition follow from naturality of the
482	  representing bijection in \(\mathcal{A}\). For the equivalence claim, \(\underline{\Spec}_X\)
483	  is fully faithful because the isomorphism
484	  \(\pi_* \mathcal{O}_{\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})} \cong \mathcal{A}\)
485	  (locally identifying with \(\Gamma(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)),\mathcal{O}) = \mathcal{A}(U)\)
486	  via Theorem~REF) reconstructs \(\mathcal{A}\) from
487	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\). Essential surjectivity onto affine \(X\)-schemes uses
488	  the canonical morphism \(X \to \underline{\Spec}_X(\pi_*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of Stacks
489	  lemma-canonical-morphism; on an affine \(X\)-scheme \(\pi : Y \to X\), this canonical
490	  morphism is an isomorphism (affine-locally it is the identity
491	  \(\Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Spec\Gamma(\pi^{-1}(U), \mathcal{O}_Y)\)).
492	\end{proof}

494	\section{Lean encoding}
495	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
496	
497	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
498	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
499	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
500	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
501	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
502	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
503	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
504	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
505	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
506	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
507	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
508	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
509	(Theorem~REF) is expressed as a
510	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
511	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
512	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
513	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
514	(Theorem~REF) and functoriality
515	(Theorem~REF) are derived from the universal property by
516	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
517	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
518

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}

196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244	
245	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as
246	% the direct application `(Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`,
247	% axiom-clean modulo the carrier-level simplification on `OnProduct`. The
248	% invertibility-preservation step (Stacks 01HH: pullback of invertible is
249	% invertible) is deferred to iter-187+ once `OnProduct` carries an
250	% invertibility witness.
251	
252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}
321	
322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368	
369	% NOTE (iter-186 review): The iter-186 Lane A.1.b prover landed the body as a
370	% `Setoid` on `OnProduct = (pullback πC πT).Modules` whose relation is
371	% `r L L' := Nonempty (L ≅ L')` (iso-class equivalence; axiom-clean). This
372	% identifies isomorphic modules (giving the underlying set of `Pic(C ×_S T)`
373	% once invertibility is imposed in iter-187+) but does NOT yet quotient by
374	% the pullback subgroup `pi_T^* Pic(T)`. The full quotient requires (a) a
375	% tensor-product structure on `Scheme.Modules` (Mathlib ships
376	% `PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj` but no monoidal structure on
377	% `Scheme.Modules` directly at b80f227), and (b) an inverse for invertible
378	% sheaves (depends on the iter-187+ `IsInvertible` predicate). Iter-187+
379	% refinement: `r L L' := exists N : T.Modules, Nonempty (L tensor L'inv iso
380	% pullbackAlongProjection πC πT N)` once both are available.
381	
382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The
385	% project-side proof is therefore a structural restatement in Lean syntax,
386	% not a translation of an external argument.
387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}

480	\section{Lean encoding}
481	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
482	
483	The Lean target file is the new
484	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
485	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
486	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
487	Lean targets as follows.
488	
489	\begin{itemize}
490	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
491	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
492	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
493	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
494	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
495	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
496	    the product scheme.
497	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
498	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
499	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
500	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
501	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
502	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
503	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
504	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
505	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
506	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
507	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
508	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
509	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
510	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
511	    structural existence of the quotient set
512	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
513	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
514	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
515	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
516	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
517	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
518	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
519	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
520	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
521	    quotient-induced map.
522	\end{itemize}
523	
524	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
525	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
526	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
527	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
528	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).

558	\section{Internal-consistency check}
559	\label{sec:lbp_consistency_check}
560	
561	The five declaration blocks form a connected \(\uses\) chain rooted in
562	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}:
563	\begin{itemize}
564	  \item \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} is a root (no internal \(\uses\));
565	    its math content uses Mathlib's invertible-module predicate (not chapter
566	    dependency).
567	  \item \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} uses
568	    \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.

1	\chapter{Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology with \(k\)-module coefficients}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_MayerVietoris}
3	
4	This chapter constructs the Mayer--Vietoris long exact sequence for sheaf cohomology valued in \(k\)-modules, and develops the \v{C}ech-acyclicity machinery for affine basic-open covers. Together these provide the categorical engine for the Serre finiteness argument (Phase~A step~6). The carriers \(H^n(X,\mathcal F)\) and \(H^{\prime n}(U,\mathcal F)\) live in Chapter~REF and are imported here.
5	
6	The chapter introduces no protected declarations: every definition, lemma, and instance here is auxiliary, supporting the construction of \(H^1(C,\mathcal O_C)\) in Definition~REF.
7	
8	\section{Functoriality of cohomology in the open variable}
9	\label{sec:hmodule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
10	
11	The cohomology-of-an-open carrier \(H^{\prime n}(X,\mathcal F)\) is contravariantly functorial in the open variable \(X\). Assembling this functoriality yields a presheaf
12	\[
13	  X \longmapsto H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F),
14	\]
15	and, varying \(\mathcal F\) as well, a bifunctor from sheaves of \(k\)-modules to presheaves of abelian groups. The codomain is the category of abelian groups because \(\Ext\) is always valued there, regardless of the linear enrichment of the source category; the per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is inherited from the reducible wrapper on \(H^{\prime n}\).
16	
17	\begin{definition}\leanok
18	[Cohomology presheaf functor, \(H'\) flavor]
19	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
20	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheafFunctor}
21	  
22	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a category equipped with a Grothendieck topology \(J\), and assume sheafification and \(\Ext\) exist for sheaves of \(k\)-modules on \(J\). For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf functor
23	  \[
24	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(k, J) \;:\; \Sheaf\,J\,(\ModuleCat\,k) \longrightarrow (C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat)
25	  \]
26	  by the standard composition of the Yoneda embedding, the free-\(k\)-module functor, sheafification, and the \(n\)-th \(\Ext\) functor.
27	\end{definition}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  
31	  \leanok
32	  The body is a direct categorical composition mirroring the general sheaf-cohomology presheaf functor. Universe pinning is required because the morphism universe of the sheaf category lives one level above the object universe.
33	\end{proof}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Cohomology presheaf, \(H'\) flavor]
37	  \label{def:Scheme_HModule_prime_cohomologyPresheaf}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule'_cohomologyPresheaf}
39	  
40	  In the setting of Definition~REF, let \(\mathcal F\) be a sheaf of \(k\)-modules. For \(n \in \mathbb N\), define the cohomology presheaf
41	  \[
42	    \mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F) \;:\; C^{\op} \longrightarrow \AddCommGrpCat
43	  \]
44	  to be the evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
45	\end{definition}
46	
47	\begin{proof}
48	  
49	  \leanok
50	  Direct evaluation of the bifunctor at \(\mathcal F\).
51	\end{proof}
52	
53	\begin{remark}
54	  For every object \(X\) of \(C\), the underlying type of \(\mathcal H^{\prime n}(\mathcal F)(X)\) is definitionally equal to \(H^{\prime n}(X, \mathcal F)\). The per-fiber \(k\)-module structure is therefore inherited through the \(H'\) wrapper.
55	\end{remark}
56	
57	\begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
58	  The cohomology presheaf functor is declared as a non-computable definition and the presheaf as a non-computable abbrev, matching the reducibility conventions of the general sheaf-cohomology API. The abbrev form is load-bearing: downstream definitional unfolding needs to see through to the fiber-level \(H'\) carrier.
59	\end{remark}

2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean

10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file

18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]

32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:quot_setup}
34	
35	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
36	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
37	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
38	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
39	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
40	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
41	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
42	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
43	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness; see~\)
44	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
45	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
46	
47	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1 and
48	% \S 5; FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
49	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500 +
50	% L2154-L2542). The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
51	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating

### Q1: `lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso` (route (c)) — complete and correct?

Glue. Apply `lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W` to get the absorption iso.

2. **Transport presheaf associator**: Apply `a.mapIso α` to the presheaf-level monoidal associator.

3. **Restore inner sheafification right**: Symmetric argument with `M^♭ ◁ η_{N⊗P}` using M locally trivial and the left unitor λ.

**Yes.** The statement is well-formed: if `(toPresheaf R₀).map f` lies in `J.W` (locally bijective), then `(sheafification α).map f` is an isomorphism.
